title: Unidirectional-like Edge Transport Induced by Non-Hermitian Skin Effects
authors: Junyi Rao, Jinting Ding, Qinghua Guo, Jun He, Xiang Ni
abstract: Non-Hermitian skin effects (NHSEs) enable dramatic boundary accumulation of waves, yet their experimental realization typically demands engineered nonreciprocity or spatially patterned loss. Here we demonstrate theoretically and experimentally that uniform loss provides a simple and previously overlooked mechanism for enforcing unidirectional-like edge transport in photonic crystals (PhCs) that breaks time-reversal symmetry in the presence of nonchiral edge states. Using a core cladding geometry where domains share identical Chern numbers but possess distinct bulk polarizations, we show that uniform loss activates NHSEs that reshape the spectral topology of edge bands, giving rise to point gap windings that dictate a one way propagation. Near field measurements confirm that loss converts intrinsically bidirectional interface states into a unidirectional-like circulation around the entire domain wall, showing excellent agreement with theory. Our results establish uniform loss as a universal and structurally simple route for achieving unidirectional-like wave transport.
summary: This research demonstrates that introducing uniform material loss into photonic crystals can induce unidirectional-like edge transport by activating Non-Hermitian Skin Effects. The theoretical model and subsequent microwave experiments confirm that this simple mechanism filters out unwanted propagation directions, offering a universal design principle for one-way waveguiding.
main_problem: The paper addresses how to achieve unidirectional-like edge transport in photonic crystals (PhCs) using a simple, universal mechanism: uniform material loss.
main_result: Uniform loss activates Non-Hermitian Skin Effects (NHSEs), which reshape the spectral topology via point-gap windings and filter the edge states into a single direction of propagation.
method: The study combines theoretical analysis using a modified tight-binding model with experimental verification using near-field microwave measurements on fabricated PhC prototypes.
model_or_system: The system is based on Photonic Crystals (PhCs) modeled by a 2D Su-Schrieffer-Heeger type Hamiltonian, involving two domains sharing Chern numbers but having distinct polarizations. The mechanism relies on introducing global, uniform material loss ($\Gamma$).
key_observables: Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), spectral winding in the complex energy plane, and directional circulation observed via near-field measurements.
important_parameters: Uniform loss strength ($\Gamma$), magnetic flux ($\phi$), and the geometry of the core-cladding structure.
main_assumptions: The method achieves unidirectional transport without requiring intrinsic topological protection; it relies solely on introducing global, uniform material dissipation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the transition from bidirectional to unidirectional transport via loss (Fig. 1), show complex band structures revealing spectral winding when both loss and flux are present (Fig. 2), and confirm directional circulation through near-field imaging of fabricated PhCs (Fig. 5).
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the theoretical mechanism using a tight-binding model to show how uniform loss induces NHSEs and spectral winding, followed by experimental validation on core-cladding photonic crystal structures demonstrating directional filtering.
why_interesting: This work provides an accessible, non-topological route to engineered wave control in photonics, which is highly relevant for designing one-way quantum waveguides or optical circuits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Transition from bi-directional to unidirectional-like interface states via global and uniform loss. (a) With a nonzero polarization difference ( 0 P ∆ ≠ ), the interface supports non-chiral interface states. (b) A uniform magnetic field lifts the band degeneracy, creating an energy splitting between counter-propagating interface states. (c) Introducing global and uniform loss activates the non- Hermitian skin effect, converting interface states into unidirectional-like transport.

Fig. 2: Energy bands of the 2D modified SSH lattice and the generalized Brillouin zone (GBZ) of the corresponding non-chiral edge states. (a) Schematic diagram of the tight-binding model. (b) Energy bands for the Hermitian case, with parameters t=γ=0.3, λ=1, ϕ=0.3π, and Γ = 0. The inset magnifies the edge bands

FIG. 4. The finite structure and band structure under global uniform loss. (a) Photograph of the fabricated PhC with top metallic plate removed: a central core of "expanded" cells is fully surrounded by a cladding of "shrunken" cells with absorbing boundaries applied along both the x and y. (b) Numerically calculated complex band structure for the finite "core-cladding" geometry. (c)-(f) Experimentally retrieved interface dispersions obtained via spatial Fourier transform of the measured complex fields along interfaces I-IV.

FIG. 5. Experimental (a) and simulated (b) spatial near-field imaging of unidirectional-like propagation. By sequentially moving the source antenna along the interface, the interface state exhibits unidirectional-like propagation along each segment, collectively forming a continuous clockwise circulation around the entire interface.
